preFix.title: Managing feather pecking in a flock
preFix.subtitle: Reducing the conditions that start pecking before it escalates to injury.
preFix.excerpt: A chicken with a bare patch on her back is not a discipline problem. It is a management signal. Feather pecking starts when birds are overcrowded, understimulated, or short of dietary protein. Catching and fixing the root cause stops it from becoming cannibalism.
preFix.sourceNotes:
DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Laying Hens (2002, Crown copyright), beak-trimming and feather-pecking sections. https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/animal-welfare-codes-of-practice
Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Chapter X on feather-eating, Internet Archive.
AHDB Pork welfare guidance adapted where applicable to laying flocks.

content.text: Feather pecking is one of the most common management problems in laying flocks. A bare back, a thin tail, or a raw patch at the base of the neck are the early signs. Left unchecked, it draws blood, and once blood is visible, other birds join in. The outcome is

is not the cause. Feather pecking almost always traces back to one or more of four conditions: overcrowding, boredom, low dietary protein, or poor light levels. The fix is to identify and remove the cause.
First step: separate any bird with blood visible

Any bird with a wound or exposed skin should come out of the flock immediately. Bees investigate red; so do chickens. Clean the wound with plain salt water and keep the bird in a separate pen until the skin has healed over. Returning a bird with even a small pink patch invites resumption.

attrs.body: The aggressor in feather pecking is often not the highest-ranking bird. She is often a mid-rank bird that has found an easier victim. Numbered leg bands on all birds let you identify which individual is responsible. If you can isolate her for a week and reintroduce at the bottom of the social order, the behaviour sometimes stops. Not always.
attrs.title: Identifying the pecker

content.text: The minimum practical stocking rate for free-range layers in an enclosed run is 4 square metres per bird. Below that, the flock is crowded and contact aggression increases. If the run is small, extend it with additional electric poultry netting. In extreme cases, thin the flock.

Bored birds peck. Hanging a cabbage or bunch of kale in the run at beak-height gives them something to work at for several hours. Scattering grain in deep litter makes them scratch and peck at the ground rather than each other. A perch set at different heights gives the flock more to do.

Feather pecking often intensifies during moult. The bird pulling feathers is partly seeking protein. Check that the flock is on a feed with at least 16 to 17 percent crude protein. Switching from pellets to mash at moult, or supplementing with fishmeal or mealworms, reduces the deficit. Cut back on supplemental extras such as corn and kitchen scraps that dilute the protein content of the ration.

High light intensity inside the coop raises aggression. Red light calms poultry and reduces pecking; pale blue or standard white artificial light increases it. If the coop has large windows, consider covering the lower third. Birds do not peck in near-darkness.
What to watch in the following weeks

Walk through the flock each morning and look at the back and tail of every bird. A well-managed flock should have no bare patches. If pecking resumes after you have addressed the four causes, the problem is likely a single persistent aggressor. Mark her with a numbered leg band, identify her, and remove her from the flock.

items.fix: Wait four to six weeks. Feathers regrowing after moult look like stiff quills before opening out. A bare patch that is dry, scabbed, or completely feather-free with no signs of regrowth is a wound, not a moult.
items.cause: The bird with bare patches may be in moult and the feathers are regrowing slowly
items.symptom: Feather pecking has stopped but feathers are not regrowing

items.fix: Remove the targeted bird temporarily and reintroduce once wounds have healed. If she is consistently at the bottom and the attacks resume, she may do better in a smaller group.
items.cause: She is at the bottom of the pecking order or has a distinctive marking the flock finds stimulating
items.symptom: One bird is being targeted by the whole flock

items.fix: Introduce new birds at dusk so they wake in the existing coop without being chased in. Provide two feeding and drinking stations so low-rank birds can access feed without confrontation. Watch closely for the first two weeks.
items.cause: The social hierarchy is being re-established; new birds are the most vulnerable
items.symptom: Pecking intensifies after adding new birds
